unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Unit 3 is called Reflections because it is all about looking closely at the world around you and expressing what you see, think, and feel through poetry and reflective writing. Poets use special tools to paint pictures with words, and by the end of this unit you will use those same tools yourself.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and tick your Word Wall.
Read the Reading section below carefully, then answer the comprehension questions.
Work through the Grammar Focus on comparatives and superlatives, then do the practice.
Complete the Writing Workshop to create your own reflective poem.
Practise the Listening and Speaking activities with your AI tutor or a partner.
Read the Story file, 'The Cracked Charter', and answer the reflection questions.
Finish with Check Your Learning to see what you have achieved.
Key Vocabulary
Here are the most important words for this unit. You will meet them in the reading, the grammar, and the writing sections. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary companion file where you will find the full definition and example sentence.
Poetry and Language Words
Story and Unit Words
These words appear in 'The Cracked Charter' and throughout the unit:
charter – a special document giving rights or rules to a group
constitution – the set of rules that explains how a country works
fracture – a break or crack in something hard
tradition – a way of doing things passed down through generations
collaborative – working together with others
legislative – related to making laws or rules
architect – a person who designs buildings and structures
artifacts – objects made by people long ago
Reading: Poetry and Reflection
In this section you will read two poems and one short reflective text. As you read, look for the figurative language tools you learned about in your vocabulary: similes, metaphors, and personification. Read each poem at least twice — once silently, and once aloud.
Poem 1: The Sea
This poem uses personification and metaphor to describe the ocean. Read it aloud slowly, feeling the rhythm of the words.
The sea is a hungry dog,
Giant and grey.
He rolls on the beach all day.
With his clashing teeth and shaggy jaws
Hour upon hour he gnaws
The rumbling, tumbling stones,
And 'Bones, bones, bones, bones!'
The giant sea-dog moans,
Licking his greasy paws.
And when the night wind roars
And the moon rocks in the stormy cloud,
He bounds to his feet and snuffs and sniffs,
Shaking his wet sides over the cliffs,
And howls and howls and howls and howls.
But on quiet days in May or June,
When even the grasses on the dune
Play no more their reedy tune,
He lies on the sandy shores,
So quiet, so quiet, he scarcely snores.
— James Reeves
After reading, think about these questions:
The poet says 'The sea is a hungry dog.' Is this a simile or a metaphor? How do you know?
Find two examples of personification in the poem. What human actions does the sea perform?
What is the tone of the poem in the second stanza? Is it calm or wild? What words tell you?
In the last stanza the sea 'scarcely snores.' What does this tell us about the sea in May or June?
What is the theme of this poem? What is the poet trying to say about the sea?
Close Reading: How to Analyse a Poem
Before we move to our next poem, let us learn a step-by-step way to analyse any poem. You can use these steps every time you read poetry:
Read it through once without stopping. Just enjoy the sound and rhythm.
Read it again slowly. This time, underline or circle words you do not know.
Identify the subject: What is the poem about? What is the poet describing?
Look for figurative language: Are there similes, metaphors, or personification? Mark them.
Think about the structure: How many stanzas are there? Do the lines rhyme? Is there a pattern?
Consider the tone: What feeling does the poem give you? Is it joyful, sad, calm, wild, thoughtful, or playful?
Find the theme: What is the poet's main message or idea? What do they want us to think about after reading?
Form your opinion: Which part did you like best? What would you ask the poet if you could meet them?
Now practise these steps with 'The Sea' before you move on. Write one sentence for each step in your notebook.
Poem 2: Haiku Collection
A haiku is a very short Japanese poem with a strict syllable pattern: 5 syllables in the first line, 7 syllables in the second, and 5 syllables in the third. Haiku usually describe nature and end with a surprising image or comparison.
Haiku 1:
Pen-guins, black and white
Sur-vi-ving in i-cy seas
State-ly em-pe-rors
Haiku 2:
Boi-ling, hot la-va
Bub-bling from the moun-tain top
Ea-ger to be free
Haiku 3:
Cool morn-ing dew drops
Spar-kling on the spi-der's web
Na-ture's dia-mond ring
After reading, think about these questions:
Count the syllables in each line of Haiku 1. Does it follow the 5–7–5 pattern?
In Haiku 2, the lava is described as 'Eager to be free.' What kind of figurative language is this?
In Haiku 3, what is being compared to a diamond ring? Is this a simile or a metaphor?
Which haiku do you like best? Write one sentence explaining why.
Try clapping the syllables of each line. Which line was hardest to count?
Reading 3: A Reflective Passage — 'Looking at the Wind'
This short reflective text is inspired by the poem 'Wind' by Christina Rossetti. Read it carefully and think about how the writer uses descriptive language.
I remember the day the wind came to our neighbourhood like an uninvited guest. It arrived without warning, lifting the dust from the streets and hurling it against our windows. The old acacia tree in our courtyard trembled and bent, as though it were bowing to a powerful king. Sheets of laundry flew from the line like startled birds.
My little sister Halimo hid under the table, her eyes wide. 'Is the wind angry?' she whispered. I told her no, the wind is not angry — it is just doing its job, pushing clouds across the sky so the rain can follow. But secretly, I thought the wind did sound angry that day. It howled between the buildings and rattled every door.
When it stopped, the world was quiet in a way I had never noticed before. The leaves lay still. The dust settled. Everything looked washed and new, as if the wind had swept the neighbourhood clean. I stepped outside and breathed in the cool air. Hooyo called me back inside for supper, but I stood there a moment longer, feeling grateful. Even something wild and frightening can leave the world more beautiful than it found it.
After reading, answer these questions:
Find one simile in the passage and explain what two things are being compared.
Find one metaphor in the passage. What is being described as something else?
Find one example of personification. What human action is given to a non-human thing?
What is the theme of this reflective passage? What lesson does the writer learn?
The writer says the wind was 'like an uninvited guest.' What does this tell you about how the wind arrived?
Why do you think the writer calls this piece a 'reflection'? What is the writer reflecting on?
Understanding the Text
Now that you have read the poems and the reflective passage, let us go deeper. Complete the activities below to show your understanding.
Good readers do not just read the words on the page. They ask questions, make connections, and think about what the writer is really saying. This is called critical reading, and it is an important skill for Year 5 and beyond. The activities below will help you practise reading critically.

visual.alt: A learner comparing poems and recording thoughtful reflections

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A plan or rule that helps people decide what to do.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Our school has a policy that everyone must wear a uniform.
practiceSentences: Our school has a policy that everyone must wear a uniform.
The library's new policy lets pupils renew their books online from home.
Should our school adopt a policy about kindness in the playground?
"Our family policy is homework first, then games," said Father firmly.
The mayor explained the city's recycling policy during her visit to our class.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - o - l - i - c - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'policy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A large area inside a building where people wait or meet.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: We waited in the lobby of the museum for our guide.
practiceSentences: We waited in the lobby of the museum for our guide.
Parents gathered in the school lobby to see the new art display.
Why is the theatre lobby so crowded before the show begins?
"Meet me by the fountain in the lobby at three o'clock," said Aunt Halima.
A friendly guard greeted every visitor who entered the marble lobby.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - o - b - b - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'lobby', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A set of rules that explains how a country or organisation works.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The constitution of our school tells us the rights and rules we follow.
practiceSentences: The constitution of our school tells us the rights and rules we follow.
The class studied how the constitution protects the rights of every citizen.
Who wrote the first constitution for this country, and when was that?
"Our club's constitution says every member gets one vote," Sami reminded the group.
Changing a constitution takes time because many people must agree first.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - n - s - t - i - t - u - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'constitution', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A special document that gives rights or rules to a group.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The school received a charter to start a new programme for students.
practiceSentences: The school received a charter to start a new programme for students.
The explorers carried a royal charter giving them permission to build a port.
Does the new sports club have a charter listing its rules yet?
"This charter lists the rights of every child in our school," said the head teacher.
Samira's scout group signed a charter promising to protect the local pond.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - h - a - r - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'charter', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Related to making laws or rules.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The legislative branch of the government makes the laws.
practiceSentences: The legislative branch of the government makes the laws.
The legislative building has a golden dome that shines above the city.
What does the legislative branch of government actually do all day?
"Our trip to the legislative assembly taught us how laws are made," said Theo.
Members of the legislative council debated the new park for three hours.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - e - g - i - s - l - a - t - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'legislative', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A special job or task that someone is trying to do.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Our class has a mission to clean up the park this weekend.
practiceSentences: Our class has a mission to clean up the park this weekend.
The fire brigade's mission is to keep every family in the town safe.
What is your team's mission for the science fair this year?
"My mission today is to finish reading this entire chapter," declared Maya.
The astronauts trained for two years before their mission to the space station.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - i - s - s - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'mission', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The special purpose or job that something has.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The function of a pencil is to help us write.
practiceSentences: The function of a pencil is to help us write.
The main function of the heart is to pump blood around the body.
What is the function of the small red button on this machine?
"Each part of a plant has its own special function," explained our science teacher.
Idris labelled every organ and wrote its function beside the diagram.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - u - n - c - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'function', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The process of something getting bigger or growing.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The expansion of the playground means we have more space to play.
practiceSentences: The expansion of the playground means we have more space to play.
The expansion of the school means two new classrooms will open next year.
Does heating a metal bar cause its expansion, or does it shrink?
"The expansion of the bakery brought wonderful smells to our whole street," laughed Mum.
Our town's rapid expansion turned quiet fields into busy new neighbourhoods.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - x - p - a - n - s - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'expansion', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Understanding what you read or hear.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: After reading the story, I answered questions to show my comprehension.
practiceSentences: After reading the story, I answered questions to show my comprehension.
Reading slowly and asking questions improves your comprehension of difficult stories.
How did you score on the comprehension exercise about the desert travellers?
"Draw a picture of the scene to check your comprehension," suggested Miss Nora.
Sami's comprehension improved once he started summarising each page in his own words.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - m - p - r - e - h - e - n - s - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'comprehension', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: What you think will happen in the future.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My expectation is that we will finish the project on time.
practiceSentences: My expectation is that we will finish the project on time.
Samira's expectation was rain, so the sunny morning surprised her completely.
What expectation do you have for the final match on Saturday?
"My expectation is that everyone will hand in neat work," said the teacher.
The film was far better than any expectation Theo had before watching it.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - x - p - e - c - t - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'expectation', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A feeling of worry or stress when things are not calm.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There was a lot of tension in the room before the big test.
practiceSentences: There was a lot of tension in the room before the big test.
You could feel the tension in the hall as the judges counted the votes.
How do slow, deep breaths help release tension before a big race?
"The tension in this story builds until the very last page," said the librarian.
A quiet apology melted the tension between the two friends at once.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - e - n - s - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'tension', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A way to measure something, or a new aspect of a situation.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The mayor said the students opened a new dimension to the problem.
practiceSentences: The mayor said the students opened a new dimension to the problem.
Measuring each dimension of the box helped us choose the right wrapping paper.
Which dimension should we measure first, the length or the width?
"Your painting adds a whole new dimension to our project," said Miss Nora.
Learning Arabic added another dimension to Maya's love of old stories.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - i - m - e - n - s - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'dimension', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A sickness caused by germs that can spread.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: If you do not wash your hands, you might get an infection.
practiceSentences: If you do not wash your hands, you might get an infection.
Washing your hands before meals helps stop infection from spreading around.
How does a doctor know if a small cut has an infection?
"Keep the plaster on so the infection cannot get any worse," said the nurse.
Theo missed a whole week of school because of an ear infection.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - n - f - e - c - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'infection', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A way of doing things passed down through generations.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Every year, my family has a tradition of making special food for harvest day.
practiceSentences: Every year, my family has a tradition of making special food for harvest day.
Baking sweet biscuits before harvest day is a beloved tradition in our family.
Which family tradition would you like to continue when you grow up?
"Our tradition of Friday storytelling started with my great-grandmother," said Grandma proudly.
The school keeps its tradition of planting a new tree every spring.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - r - a - d - i - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'tradition', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Keeping someone or something safe from harm.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The protective glass around the charter keeps it safe.
practiceSentences: The protective glass around the charter keeps it safe.
Cyclists wear protective helmets to keep their heads safe in a fall.
Why do welders need to wear protective masks over their faces?
"Put on your protective goggles before we mix the liquids," instructed Mr Ahmed.
The protective mother hen spread her wings over her chicks in the rain.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - o - t - e - c - t - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'protective', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Very simple and not advanced or developed.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The primitive tools used by early humans were made from stones.
practiceSentences: The primitive tools used by early humans were made from stones.
Early humans used primitive tools made from sharpened stones and bones.
How did primitive oil lamps work before electricity was discovered?
"This primitive raft carried people across rivers thousands of years ago," said the museum guide.
Compared with modern phones, my grandfather's first radio seems quite primitive.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - r - i - m - i - t - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'primitive', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Wanting to win or be better than others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She is very competitive and always tries to do her best.
practiceSentences: She is very competitive and always tries to do her best.
Sami is so competitive that he practises his spelling lists during breakfast.
Is your sister as competitive at chess as she is at running?
"Stay competitive, but always shake hands after the match," reminded the coach.
The competitive bakers each tried to make the tallest cake at the fair.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - m - p - e - t - i - t - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'competitive', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A different choice or option.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: If you do not like the first idea, we can find an alternative.
practiceSentences: If you do not like the first idea, we can find an alternative.
When the lift broke, the stairs were the only alternative for reaching the library.
Can you suggest an alternative ending for the story we read today?
"Solar power is a clean alternative to burning coal," explained the visiting scientist.
Maya chose the alternative route home because the main road was flooded.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - l - t - e - r - n - a - t - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'alternative', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Working together with others to achieve something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The students used a collaborative approach to create their mural.
practiceSentences: The students used a collaborative approach to create their mural.
The mural by the gate was a collaborative effort by three different classes.
What makes a collaborative project succeed when working alone might fail?
"This collaborative story needs one sentence from every pupil," announced Miss Nora.
Their collaborative science model won first prize at the regional fair.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - l - l - a - b - o - r - a - t - i - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'collaborative', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A very small version of something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I have a miniature model of a car that fits in my hand.
practiceSentences: I have a miniature model of a car that fits in my hand.
Grandfather built a miniature town hall from matchsticks over one long winter.
Would you like to visit the museum's famous miniature railway this weekend?
"My miniature garden fits inside an old teacup," said Samira proudly.
The dollhouse kitchen even has miniature pots no bigger than buttons.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - i - n - i - a - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'miniature', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A break or crack in something hard, like bone or stone.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: There was a fracture in the old charter running through the page.
practiceSentences: There was a fracture in the old charter running through the page.
The X-ray showed a small fracture in Theo's wrist after his fall.
Can a tiny fracture in a dam really cause a huge flood?
"This fracture in the rock was made by ice over many winters," said the geologist.
Doctors put a cast on the fracture so the bone could heal straight.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - r - a - c - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'fracture', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The way you hold your body when you sit or stand.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Good posture means sitting up straight in your chair.
practiceSentences: Good posture means sitting up straight in your chair.
Good posture while reading keeps your neck and back from aching.
How does sitting with straight posture help you breathe more easily?
"Check your posture before you begin writing," Miss Nora reminds us daily.
The gymnast's perfect posture made her routine look effortless and graceful.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - o - s - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'posture', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A large bird that eats dead animals.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The vulture circled high in the sky, looking for food.
practiceSentences: The vulture circled high in the sky, looking for food.
A vulture can glide for hours on warm air without flapping its wings.
Why does the vulture wait so patiently in the tall dead tree?
"Look, a vulture is circling high above the far hills!" shouted Idris.
The nature film showed a vulture cleaning the plains by eating leftovers.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: v - u - l - t - u - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'vulture', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Objects made by people a long time ago.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The museum has many artifacts from ancient civilisations.
practiceSentences: The museum has many artifacts from ancient civilisations.
Archaeologists carefully brushed the sand away from the buried artifacts.
Which artifacts in the museum are more than a thousand years old?
"These artifacts tell us how ancient families cooked and traded," said the guide.
Sami photographed the golden artifacts displayed in the glass cabinet.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - r - t - i - f - a - c - t - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'artifacts', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who designs buildings and structures.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The architect drew a picture of the new school before it was built.
practiceSentences: The architect drew a picture of the new school before it was built.
An architect drew careful plans for the new wing of our school.
What does an architect think about when designing a grand museum?
"I want to be an architect and design beautiful libraries," announced Maya.
The famous architect used arches and light to make the hall feel peaceful.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - r - c - h - i - t - e - c - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'architect', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A comparison using 'like' or 'as'.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Her smile was as bright as the sun.
practiceSentences: Her smile was as bright as the sun.
Samira's poem used a simile comparing the moon to a silver coin.
Can you spot the simile hidden in the second verse of the poem?
"A good simile paints a picture in the reader's mind," said Miss Nora.
My favourite simile says the desert wind howls like a lonely wolf.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - i - m - i - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'simile', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Saying one thing is another to compare them.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The sea is a hungry dog.
practiceSentences: The sea is a hungry dog.
The poet's metaphor called the classroom a beehive buzzing with ideas.
What metaphor would you choose to describe a rolling thunderstorm?
"A metaphor says something is another thing, without using like or as," explained the teacher.
Theo wrote a metaphor describing his grandmother's hands as gentle maps.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - e - t - a - p - h - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'metaphor', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Giving human qualities to non-living things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The wind hurled dirt across the road.
practiceSentences: The wind hurled dirt across the road.
The poem uses personification when it says the kettle sang all morning.
Where can you find personification in the verse about the angry sea?
"Personification gives the tired old house a voice in this story," said the librarian.
Maya loved the personification of the wind whispering secrets to the trees.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - e - r - s - o - n - i - f - i - c - a - t - i - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'personification', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A unit of sound with one vowel sound.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The word 'ha-i-ku' has three syllables.
practiceSentences: The word 'ha-i-ku' has three syllables.
Clap your hands once for every syllable when we read the poem together.
Which syllable in 'banana' sounds the strongest when you say it aloud?
"Stress the first syllable in 'PENcil', not the second," explained Miss Nora.
Sami tapped his desk softly for each syllable of his own name.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - y - l - l - a - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'syllable', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A word with a similar meaning to another word.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A synonym for 'gnaw' is 'chew'.
practiceSentences: A synonym for 'gnaw' is 'chew'.
A synonym for 'happy' is 'joyful', and both fit nicely in your poem.
Can you give me a synonym for 'enormous' before the timer rings?
"Use a synonym instead of repeating 'nice' five times," advised the teacher.
Samira's word wall pairs each new spelling with a helpful synonym.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - y - n - o - n - y - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'synonym', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Reflections.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Identify and explain similes, metaphors, and personification in poems.
Read and understand different kinds of poetry, including haiku.
Count syllables and understand how poets use rhythm and sound.
Tell the difference between literal and figurative language.
Describe the theme and tone of a poem in your own words.
Use comparatives and superlatives correctly in sentences.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write your own reflective poem or piece of reflective writing.

readings.title: Reflections: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Unit 3 is called Reflections because it is all about looking closely at the world around you and expressing what you see, think, and feel through poetry and reflective writing. Poets use special tools to paint pictures with words, and by the end of this unit you will use those same tools yourself.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Identify and explain similes, metaphors, and personification in poems.
Read and understand different kinds of poetry, including haiku.
Count syllables and understand how poets use rhythm and sound.
Tell the difference between literal and figurative language.
Describe the theme and tone of a poem in your own words.
Use comparatives and superlatives correctly in sentences.
Write your own reflective poem or piece of reflective writing.
Perform a poem aloud with expression, and give helpful feedback to a classmate.
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few words each day and tick your Word Wall.
Read the Reading section below carefully, then answer the comprehension questions.
Work through the Grammar Focus on comparatives and superlatives, then do the practice.
Complete the Writing Workshop to create your own reflective poem.
Practise the Listening and Speaking activities with your AI tutor or a partner.
Read the Story file, 'The Cracked Charter', and answer the reflection questions.
Finish with Check Your Learning to see what you have achieved.
Here are the most important words for this unit. You will meet them in the reading, the grammar, and the writing sections. If any word is new, look it up in your Vocabulary companion file where you will find the full definition and example sentence.
These words appear in 'The Cracked Charter' and throughout the unit:
charter – a special document giving rights or rules to a group
constitution – the set of rules that explains how a country works
fracture – a break or crack in something hard
tradition – a way of doing things passed down through generations

readings.title: Reflections: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
collaborative – working together with others
legislative – related to making laws or rules
architect – a person who designs buildings and structures
artifacts – objects made by people long ago
In this section you will read two poems and one short reflective text. As you read, look for the figurative language tools you learned about in your vocabulary: similes, metaphors, and personification. Read each poem at least twice — once silently, and once aloud.
This poem uses personification and metaphor to describe the ocean. Read it aloud slowly, feeling the rhythm of the words.
The sea is a hungry dog,
He rolls on the beach all day.
With his clashing teeth and shaggy jaws
And when the night wind roars
And the moon rocks in the stormy cloud,
He bounds to his feet and snuffs and sniffs,
Shaking his wet sides over the cliffs,
And howls and howls and howls and howls.
But on quiet days in May or June,
When even the grasses on the dune
Play no more their reedy tune,
He lies on the sandy shores,
So quiet, so quiet, he scarcely snores.
Close Reading: How to Analyse a Poem
Before we move to our next poem, let us learn a step-by-step way to analyse any poem. You can use these steps every time you read poetry:
Read it through once without stopping. Just enjoy the sound and rhythm.
Read it again slowly. This time, underline or circle words you do not know.
Look for figurative language: Are there similes, metaphors, or personification? Mark them.
Now practise these steps with 'The Sea' before you move on. Write one sentence for each step in your notebook.
A haiku is a very short Japanese poem with a strict syllable pattern: 5 syllables in the first line, 7 syllables in the second, and 5 syllables in the third. Haiku usually describe nature and end with a surprising image or comparison.

readings.title: The Cracked Charter, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
In a township near Cape Town, an old courthouse stood beside a crumbling museum. The walls were dusty, and the sign outside was faded. But inside, a class of Year 5 students stood in awe. They had come for a history project, but they were about to solve something unexpected.
Their teacher, Mr. Idris, led them into a quiet hall. “This building once held debates,” he said. “Here, people argued about policy, voted on rules, and wrote early drafts of our town’s constitution.”
Amal walked slowly across the tiled floor. “It smells like paper and time,” she whispered.
Amal pointed to a case in the centre of the room. “Look! There’s the original town charter.”
It was yellowed with age, sealed in glass.
Noah leaned closer. “There’s a fracture in it,” he said. “A line running right through the page!”
“That’s strange,” Mr. Idris said. “Let’s ask the curator.”
They met Mrs. Idris, the museum guide, in the lobby.
“Why is there a crack in the charter?” asked Nadia.
Mrs. Idris smiled. “That’s part of the story. Some say the town council had a fracture in trust. A disagreement broke unity the day the charter was signed.”
Amal raised her hand. “Can we study it for our project?”
“You may,” she said. “But treat it with care — it’s an artifact, and very protective glass surrounds it.”
The students began to sketch the charter and read the labels around it.
Suddenly, Nadia gasped. “This stamp… it’s not from our town!”
Mr. Idris’s eyes narrowed. “That’s right. This symbol is legislative — it belonged to the regional capital, not our township.”
“Why would their symbol be on our charter?” asked Idris.

readings.title: The Cracked Charter, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Mrs. Idris nodded slowly. “Now that is a question worth asking.”
The next week, the students turned the mystery into a class mission.
They split into groups. One researched the history of the local government. Another studied the roles of architects who had helped design the early miniature courthouse.
“Look,” Nadia said. “One of the architects lobbied for a bigger hall — he wanted more public voices in meetings.”
“But the mayor at the time preferred small rooms,” said Idris. “He believed public tension could lead to infection — social unrest.”
“That’s a weak argument,” said Nadia. “Fear of expansion isn’t a reason to silence people.”
Their teacher nodded. “Use that in your report. That’s an opinion backed by comprehension of history.”
At the presentation day, the class decorated their wall with maps, notes, and a collaborative mural showing voices breaking through a cracked wall.
Amal explained, “We believe the fracture in the charter is both physical and symbolic. It shows the fight between voices wanting tradition and others asking for change.”
Noah added, “Some feared alternative policies. But over time, people function better when ideas are shared.”
They used comparatives to explain how modern policies are “more inclusive than early ones” and said the current constitution is “the most protective we’ve had.”
That afternoon, the mayor visited. She smiled and said, “You’ve opened a new dimension to an old problem.”
Amal asked shyly, “Can we suggest ideas for a new youth charter?”
“You may,” the mayor said. “Write one. Let it reflect your generation’s hopes.”
Over the next week, the class wrote together.
Amal typed:
“We believe no one is too small to speak.
Our mission is to learn, listen, and lead.”

readings.title: The Cracked Charter, part 3
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Nadia added:
“We will value all traditions — but not let them block progress.”
Noah closed with:
“Our system must be fairer, clearer, and more competitive, not in conflict — but in courage.”
They titled it: “The Students’ Charter of Hope.”
On the final day, the mayor returned. The students stood straight, their posture showing pride.
“You’ve written a beautiful document,” she said. “One day, some of you may serve in our legislative halls.”
“And I’ll build them,” said Nadia. “I want to be an architect.”
“And I’ll protect them,” said Adam. “Like a vulture guarding its hill.”
“You function well as a team,” the mayor said. “And that’s the most powerful kind of constitution.”
As they left, the cracked charter remained in its case — but now, it had new meaning. It was no longer just a broken page. It was a lesson.
A reminder that even fractures can lead to better foundations.

grammar.title: What Are Comparatives and Superlatives?
grammar.explanation:
Why They Matter
Comparatives and superlatives are special forms of adjectives. They help us compare people, places, things, and ideas. When we compare poems, stories, characters, or events, we use these forms to say which is bigger, better, more beautiful, or most exciting.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Why They Matter
Comparatives and superlatives are special forms of adjectives. They help us compare people, places, things, and ideas. When we compare poems, stories, characters, or events, we use these forms to say which is bigger, better, more beautiful, or most exciting.
There are two types:
A comparative compares two things: 'A haiku is shorter than a ballad.'
A superlative compares three or more things: 'She is the best poet in our class.'
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply what are comparatives and superlatives? in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Forming Comparatives
grammar.explanation:
Rule 1: Short Adjectives (One Syllable)
Add -er to the end of the adjective.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Rule 1: Short Adjectives (One Syllable)
Add -er to the end of the adjective.
tall → taller — 'The tree is taller than the house.'
fast → faster — 'Nadia ran faster than Adam.'
old → older — 'The charter is older than the museum.'
Spelling note: If the adjective ends in one vowel + one consonant, double the last consonant: big → bigger, hot → hotter, sad → sadder.
Rule 2: Adjectives Ending in -y
Change the y to i and add -er.
happy → happier — 'She felt happier after the presentation.'
easy → easier — 'The second exercise was easier than the first.'
funny → funnier — 'His joke was funnier than mine.'
Rule 3: Longer Adjectives (Two or More Syllables)
Use more before the adjective. Do not add -er.
beautiful → more beautiful — 'The sunset was more beautiful than yesterday’s.'
inclusive → more inclusive — 'Our rules are more inclusive than the old ones.'
competitive → more competitive — 'The debate was more competitive this year.'
Always use than after the comparative when naming both things being compared:
'Poetry is more creative than a report.'
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply forming comparatives in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Forming Superlatives
grammar.explanation:
Rule 1: Short Adjectives (One Syllable)
Add -est to the end and put the before it.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Rule 1: Short Adjectives (One Syllable)
Add -est to the end and put the before it.
tall → the tallest — 'She is the tallest girl in the class.'
fast → the fastest — 'He is the fastest runner in the school.'
old → the oldest — 'That is the oldest building in town.'
Spelling note: Same doubling rule applies: big → the biggest, hot → the hottest.
Rule 2: Adjectives Ending in -y
Change the y to i and add -est. Put the before it.
happy → the happiest — 'That was the happiest day of my life.'
easy → the easiest — 'This was the easiest question on the test.'
pretty → the prettiest — 'She wore the prettiest dress.'
Rule 3: Longer Adjectives (Two or More Syllables)
Use the most before the adjective. Do not add -est.
beautiful → the most beautiful — 'That is the most beautiful poem I have ever read.'
protective → the most protective — 'Our constitution is the most protective we have had.'
exciting → the most exciting — 'That was the most exciting presentation.'
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply forming superlatives in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Irregular Comparatives and Superlatives
grammar.explanation:
Some common adjectives do not follow the regular rules. You need to memorise them:
Examples in sentences:
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Some common adjectives do not follow the regular rules. You need to memorise them:
Examples in sentences:
'She is a better writer than I am.' (not 'gooder')
'That was the worst storm in years.' (not 'the most bad')
'He has more books than anyone.' (not 'mucher')
'She ate the least food at dinner.' (not 'the most little')
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply irregular comparatives and superlatives in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Common Mistakes and How to Fix Them
grammar.explanation:
Double comparative: Do not use -er and more together.
• Wrong: 'She is more taller than her sister.'
• Correct: 'She is taller than her sister.'
Missing 'the' in superlatives: Always use 'the' before a superlative.
• Wrong: 'He is fastest runner.'
• Correct: 'He is the fastest runner.'
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Double comparative: Do not use -er and more together.
• Wrong: 'She is more taller than her sister.'
• Correct: 'She is taller than her sister.'
Missing 'the' in superlatives: Always use 'the' before a superlative.
• Wrong: 'He is fastest runner.'
• Correct: 'He is the fastest runner.'
Using -est with long adjectives: Do not add -est to long adjectives.
• Wrong: 'This is the beautifulest poem.'
• Correct: 'This is the most beautiful poem.'
Wrong irregular form: Do not add -er/-est to irregular adjectives.
• Wrong: 'My drawing is gooder than yours.'
• Correct: 'My drawing is better than yours.'
Forgetting 'than' in comparatives: Include 'than' when naming both things.
• Wrong: 'She is taller me.'
• Correct: 'She is taller than me.'
Practice Exercises
Exercise 1: Choose the Correct Form
Circle or write the correct word in brackets:
A camel is (bigger / more bigger) than a goat.
That was (the most exciting / the excitingest) story I have ever read.
My sister is (more careful / carefuler) than I am.
This is (the worst / the baddest) meal I have ever tasted.
Her poem is (beautifuler / more beautiful) than mine.
He is (the fastest / the most fast) boy in our class.
Today is (hotter / more hot) than yesterday.
She is (the happiest / the most happy) person I know.
Exercise 2: Complete the Sentence
Write the correct comparative or superlative form of the adjective in brackets:
This book is __________ (interesting) than the last one.
Mount Kilimanjaro is __________ (tall) mountain in Africa.
My brother's handwriting is __________ (neat) than mine.
She is __________ (good) at maths than at English.
That was __________ (bad) film I have ever watched.
A cheetah is __________ (fast) land animal.
This exercise is __________ (easy) than Exercise 1.
He told __________ (funny) joke in the whole school.
Exercise 3: Spot and Fix the Mistake
Each sentence has one mistake. Rewrite the sentence correctly:
'She is more smarter than her brother.'
'This is the most biggest building in our town.'
'He is fastest player on the team.'
'My cat is more friendlier than my dog.'
'That was the goodest day of my life.'
'The red car is expensiver than the blue one.'
Exercise 4: Write Your Own Sentences
Write one sentence for each instruction:
Use a comparative to compare two fruits.
Use a superlative to say which animal is the best.
Use the irregular comparative of good in a sentence.
Use the irregular superlative of bad in a sentence.
Use more + a long adjective to compare two places.
Use the most + a long adjective to describe your favourite poem.
Exercise 5: Comparatives and Superlatives in 'The Cracked Charter'
Read these sentences from the story. Underline the comparative or superlative and say which type it is:
'Modern policies are more inclusive than early ones.'
'The current constitution is the most protective we have had.'
'Our system must be fairer, clearer, and more competitive.'
'That is the most powerful kind of constitution.'
grammar.commonMistake: Check meaning, sentence structure and punctuation before accepting an answer.
grammar.memoryTip: Notice the pattern, say it aloud, apply it, then reread.
grammar.practice: Apply common mistakes and how to fix them in six connected sentences, then revise two choices for clarity.

grammar.title: Grade 5 Editing and Application
grammar.explanation: Strong writers apply unit grammar while revising complete paragraphs for meaning, accuracy and flow.
grammar.ruleAndExamples: Read a paragraph aloud. Check sentence completeness, agreement, tense, joining words and punctuation. Revise one feature at a time.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: changing words without checking whether the paragraph still means the same thing. Fix: explain the purpose of every revision.
grammar.practice: Edit a two-paragraph response using the unit language focus. Mark four changes and explain how two changes improve clarity.

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Choral Verse Performance
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one of the three stanzas of 'The Sea'. Practise reading it aloud with expression. Think about:
Volume: When should your voice be loud? When should it be soft?
Speed: Should you read the stormy stanza quickly or slowly?
Tone: What feeling do you want the listener to have?
If you are in a group, divide the stanzas between you. Practise reading the whole poem together as choral verse — that means reading aloud as a group, like a choir.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise choral verse performance and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: Poetry Interview
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Imagine you could interview the poet James Reeves. With a partner or your AI tutor, take turns being the interviewer and the poet. Here are some starter questions:
Why did you compare the sea to a dog?
Which stanza is your favourite, and why?
What advice would you give a young poet writing their first poem?
Is there a sea near where you grew up? How did it make you feel?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise poetry interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Present Your Poem
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
When your haiku or simile poem is finished, practise presenting it to an audience. This could be your family, your study partner, or your AI tutor. Before you present, prepare by thinking about these questions:
What is the title of your poem, and why did you choose it?
What inspired you to write about this topic?
Which line are you most proud of, and why?
What kind of figurative language did you use?
When you present, speak clearly and slowly. Make eye contact with your audience. After reading your poem aloud, invite questions. This is excellent practice for building confidence in English speaking.
Listening Activity 2: Compare Two Readings
Ask your AI tutor to read the first stanza of 'The Sea' in two different ways: first in a calm, quiet voice, and then in a loud, dramatic voice. After listening:
Which reading felt more like the poem? Why?
How does the way a poem is read change its tone?
Which style did you prefer? Explain your choice.
Listening Activity 3: Haiku Dictation
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise present your poem and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: Reflective and Poetry Writing
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Now it is your turn to write! In this section you will create your own reflective poem and a short piece of reflective writing. Take your time, draft first, and then edit. Good writers always revise their work.
What is reflective writing? Reflective writing is when you describe an experience, a memory, or something you have observed, and then explain what you thought and felt about it. It is personal — you use the word 'I' and share your own honest reactions. The reflective passage 'Looking at the Wind' is a good example. The writer described the wind, told us how they felt, and then shared a bigger thought about how something scary can also be beautiful.
What makes a good poem? A good poem does not have to be long. It should choose its words carefully, create a picture or feeling in the reader's mind, and leave the reader thinking. Use figurative language to make your poem more powerful, but do not force it — every simile and metaphor should feel natural and true to what you are describing.
Task 1: Write a Haiku
Write your own haiku about something in nature. Remember the rules:
Line 1: 5 syllables — describe what you see (literal).
Line 2: 7 syllables — add context or action.
Line 3: 5 syllables — end with a surprising image or comparison (figurative).
Example topics: the sea, the wind, a sunset, a bird, rain, the moon, a tree.
Draft your haiku here, then count the syllables by clapping:
Line 1 (5): _______________________________
Line 2 (7): _______________________________
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 5 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about reflective and poetry writing.

activities.title: Activities 1: Figurative Language Hunt
activities.instructionsAndItems: Copy and complete this table in your notebook. Find one example of each type from the texts you have read:
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Comparing Poems
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Think about 'The Sea' and the haiku collection. Answer these questions in full sentences:
How is 'The Sea' different from a haiku in length and structure?
Which poem uses more figurative language? Give an example from each.
Both describe nature. How does the tone of 'The Sea' compare with the tone of Haiku 3?
Which style of poem would you prefer to write yourself, and why?
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Theme and Tone Chart
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Copy this chart into your notebook and fill in each row based on the texts you read:
After completing the chart, write a paragraph comparing the theme and tone of any two texts. Use at least one comparative in your paragraph, for example: 'The tone of The Sea is wilder than the tone of Haiku 3.'
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Vocabulary in Context
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Use context clues from the reading to explain what these words mean. Then check your Vocabulary file:
gnaws (from 'The Sea')
stately (from Haiku 1)
hurling (from 'Looking at the Wind')
trembled (from 'Looking at the Wind')
scarcely (from 'The Sea')
Grammar Focus: Comparatives and Superlatives
In this unit, your main grammar topic is comparatives and superlatives. These are special forms of adjectives that help us compare things. You will use them when you compare poems, characters, and ideas in your writing.
What Are Comparatives?
We use a comparative adjective to compare two things. It tells us that one thing has more or less of a quality than the other.
One-syllable adjectives: add -er. Example: tall → taller
Two-syllable adjectives ending in -y: change y to i and add -er. Example: happy → happier
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion

quizzes.question: What does 'policy' mean?
quizzes.options: A plan or rule that helps people decide what to do. | A set of rules that explains how a country or organisation works. | A special document that gives rights or rules to a group. | Related to making laws or rules.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A plan or rule that helps people decide what to do.
quizzes.explanation: Our school has a policy that everyone must wear a uniform.

quizzes.question: What does 'lobby' mean?
quizzes.options: A large area inside a building where people wait or meet. | A special document that gives rights or rules to a group. | A special job or task that someone is trying to do. | Related to making laws or rules.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A large area inside a building where people wait or meet.
quizzes.explanation: We waited in the lobby of the museum for our guide.

quizzes.question: What does 'constitution' mean?
quizzes.options: A set of rules that explains how a country or organisation works. | A special job or task that someone is trying to do. | Related to making laws or rules. | The special purpose or job that something has.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A set of rules that explains how a country or organisation works.
quizzes.explanation: The constitution of our school tells us the rights and rules we follow.

quizzes.question: What does 'charter' mean?
quizzes.options: A special document that gives rights or rules to a group. | A special job or task that someone is trying to do. | The process of something getting bigger or growing. | The special purpose or job that something has.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A special document that gives rights or rules to a group.
quizzes.explanation: The school received a charter to start a new programme for students.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Cracked Charter, part 1': “Here, people argued about _____, voted on rules, and wrote early drafts of our town’s constitution.” Amal walked slowly across the tiled floor.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Here, people argued about policy, voted on rules, and wrote early drafts of our town’s constitution.” Amal walked slowly across the tiled floor.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Cracked Charter, part 1': Idris, the museum guide, in the _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Idris, the museum guide, in the lobby.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Cracked Charter, part 3': “And that’s the most powerful kind of _____.” As they left, the cracked charter remained in its case — but now, it had new meaning.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “And that’s the most powerful kind of constitution.” As they left, the cracked charter remained in its case — but now, it had new meaning.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Reflections: source text 1': Read the Story file, 'The Cracked _____', and answer the reflection questions.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Read the Story file, 'The Cracked Charter', and answer the reflection questions.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Cracked Charter, part 1': This symbol is _____ — it belonged to the regional capital, not our township.” “Why would their symbol be on our charter?” asked Adam.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: This symbol is legislative — it belonged to the regional capital, not our township.” “Why would their symbol be on our charter?” asked Adam.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Cracked Charter, part 2': “Now that is a question worth asking.” The next week, the students turned the mystery into a class _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Now that is a question worth asking.” The next week, the students turned the mystery into a class mission.

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A plan or rule that helps people decide what to do. Our school has a policy that everyone must wear a uniform.
A large area inside a building where people wait or meet. We waited in the lobby of the museum for our guide.
A set of rules that explains how a country or organisation works. The constitution of our school tells us the rights and rules we follow.
A special document that gives rights or rules to a group. The school received a charter to start a new programme for students.
Related to making laws or rules. The legislative branch of the government makes the laws.
A special job or task that someone is trying to do. Our class has a mission to clean up the park this weekend.
The special purpose or job that something has. The function of a pencil is to help us write.
The process of something getting bigger or growing. The expansion of the playground means we have more space to play.
Understanding what you read or hear. After reading the story, I answered questions to show my comprehension.
What you think will happen in the future. My expectation is that we will finish the project on time.
